perturbation.original_text: 16.4 Bank of America shall pay Supplier for all services and applicable taxes invoiced In arrears in accordance with the terms of this Agreement, within sixty (60) calendar days of the date of receipt of a valid and correct invoice by Bank of America. Bank of America reserves the right to pay prior to the expiration of the sixty (60) day period. If Bank of America pays within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt of a valid invoice by Bank of America, a discount of two percent (2%) will be subtracted from the total invoice amount for Services.
perturbation.changed_text: 16.4 Bank of America shall pay Supplier for all services and applicable taxes invoiced In arrears in accordance with the terms of this Agreement, within ninety (90) calendar days of the date of receipt of a valid and correct invoice by Bank of America. Bank of America reserves the right to pay prior to the expiration of the ninety (90) day period. If Bank of America pays within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt of a valid invoice by Bank of America, a discount of two percent (2%) will be subtracted from the total invoice amount for Services.
perturbation.explanation: The change modifies the payment deadline from 60 to 90 days. This creates a contradiction with Section 16.5, which refers to amounts not invoiced within three months (approximately 90 days) as potentially unpayable. This introduces ambiguity regarding the enforceability of invoices close to the 90-day mark.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 16.5 Unless otherwise agreed upon by Bank of America, (i) all charges for Maintenance Services shall be invoiced in accordance. with the terms specified in the applicable Order, (ii) charges for Software shall be invoiced on the Acceptance Date, and (iii) all other charges shall be invoiced when incurred. Invoices shall contain such detail as Bank of America may reasonably require from time to time. Amounts not invoiced by Supplier to Bank of America within three (3) months after such amounts could first be invoiced under this Agreement may not thereafter be invoiced, and Bank of America shall not be required to pay such amounts.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment deadline from 60 to 90 days. Section 16.5 states that amounts not invoiced within three months (approximately 90 days) may not be payable. With the original 60-day payment term, there was a buffer between the payment deadline and the invoice submission deadline. The change makes the payment deadline of 90 days potentially overlap with the invoice submission deadline, creating ambiguity and a contradiction. The two sections now directly conflict, as invoices could be validly submitted but then deemed unpayable if not invoiced precisely at the 90-day mark. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Bank of America may terminate this Agreement, an Order and/or any Customization Schedule(s) for its convenience, without cause, at any time without further charge or expense upon at least forty-five (45) calendar days prior written notice to Supplier. Termination of one Order shall not cause a termination of this Agreement or any other Order, unless otherwise specified by Bank of America.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Bank of America may terminate this Agreement, an Order and/or any Customization Schedule(s) for its convenience, without cause, at any time without further charge or expense upon at least fifteen (15) calendar days prior written notice to Supplier. Termination of one Order shall not cause a termination of this Agreement or any other Order, unless otherwise specified by Bank of America.
perturbation.explanation: The change reduces the prior written notice period for termination by Bank of America from 45 to 15 calendar days. This creates a direct in-text contradiction with section 5.6 (a), where a full refund of license fees, Maintenance Fees and other fees paid hereunder is provided if Bank of America terminates any Product License Schedule for material default by Supplier prior to the Acceptance Date of the Software, within thirty (30) calendar days after notice of termination. Therefore, making the termination of a Product License Schedule with 15 days prior written notice more likely than the timeframe mentioned, causing the potential that this action would cause confusion
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.6 In addition to the rights of Bank of America set forth in this Section, (a) If Bank of America terminates any Product License Schedule for material default by Supplier prior to the Acceptance Date of the Software, Bank of America shall be entitled to a full refund, within thirty (30) calendar days after notice of termination, of all license fees, Maintenance Fees and other fees paid Proprietary to Bank of America Page 7 vTIP2010 hereunder; and (b) Bank of America may terminate Maintenance Services under any Product License Schedule or Order for convenience at any time, and Bank of America shall then have no obligation to pay any additional Maintenance Fees, other than for Maintenance Services performed through the date of termination. Bank of America may terminate the Maintenance Services under any Product License Schedule or Order for material default by Supplier, upon Bank of America's termination of such Maintenance Services for default, Bank of America shall be entitled to a pro rata refund of all prepaid Maintenance Fees for the period after the date of termination.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for termination by Bank of America from 45 to 15 days. This creates a direct contradiction with section 5.6(a), which requires a full refund within 30 days after notice of termination for material default by the supplier prior to the Acceptance Date. The shorter notice period makes it more likely that Bank of America could terminate with less than 30 days notice, creating a clear inconsistency in the application of the refund policy. It falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction as it creates a conflict about a deadline.

perturbation.original_text: 39.1 Bank of America will own exclusively all Work Product and Supplier hereby assigns to Bank of America all right, title and interest (including all Intellectual Property Rights) in the Work Product. Work Product, to the extent permitted by law, shall be deemed "works made for hire" (as that term is defined in the United States Copyright Act). Supplier shall provide Bank of America upon request with all assistance reasonably required to register, perfect or enforce such right, title and interest, including providing pertinent information and, executing all applications, specifications, oaths, assignments and all other instruments that Bank of America shall deem necessary. Supplier shall enter into agreements with all of its Representatives and Subcontractors necessary to establish Bank of America's sole ownership in the Work Product. Bank of America acknowledges Supplier's and its licensors' claims of proprietary rights in preexisting works of authorship and other intellectual property ("Pre-existing IP") Supplier uses in its work pursuant to this Agreement. Bank of America does not claim any right not expressly granted by this Agreement in such Pre-existing IP, which shall not be deemed Work Product, even if incorporated with Work Product in the Product Supplier delivers to Bank of America. Unless otherwise agreed in an Order, Supplier grants Bank of America a perpetual, worldwide, irrevocable, nonexclusive royalty free license to any Pre-existing IP embedded in the Work Product, which shall permit Bank of America and any transferee or sublicensee of Bank of America, subject to the restrictions in this Agreement, to make, use, import, reproduce, display, distribute, make derivative works and modify such Pre-existing IP as necessary or desirable for the use of the Work Product.
perturbation.changed_text: 39.1 Bank of America will own exclusively all Work Product and Supplier hereby assigns to Bank of America all right, title and interest (including all Intellectual Property Rights) in the Work Product. Work Product, to the extent permitted by law, shall be deemed "works made for hire" (as that term is defined in the United States Copyright Act). Supplier shall provide Bank of America upon request with all assistance reasonably required to register, perfect or enforce such right, title and interest, including providing pertinent information and, executing all applications, specifications, oaths, assignments and all other instruments that Bank of America shall deem necessary. Supplier shall enter into agreements with all of its Representatives and Subcontractors necessary to establish Bank of America's sole ownership in the Work Product. Bank of America acknowledges Supplier's and its licensors' claims of proprietary rights in preexisting works of authorship and other intellectual property ("Pre-existing IP") Supplier uses in its work pursuant to this Agreement. Bank of America claims all rights of the Pre-existing IP used in its work. Unless otherwise agreed in an Order, Supplier grants Bank of America a perpetual, worldwide, irrevocable, nonexclusive royalty free license to any Pre-existing IP embedded in the Work Product, which shall permit Bank of America and any transferee or sublicensee of Bank of America, subject to the restrictions in this Agreement, to make, use, import, reproduce, display, distribute, make derivative works and modify such Pre-existing IP as necessary or desirable for the use of the Work Product.
perturbation.explanation: The change removes acknowledgement to Supplier's and its licensors' claims of proprietary rights in pre-existing IP, and indicates that Bank of America claims all rights to Pre-existing IP. This creates a direct in-text contradiction and conflicts to the text after. The statement says that 'Bank of America claims all rights of the Pre-existing IP used in its work.' and it contradicts the final sentence of section 39.1 that says 'Supplier grants Bank of America a perpetual, worldwide, irrevocable, nonexclusive royalty free license to any Pre-existing IP embedded in the Work Product'. This causes a legal issue
perturbation.contradicted_text: 39.1 Bank of America will own exclusively all Work Product and Supplier hereby assigns to Bank of America all right, title and interest (including all Intellectual Property Rights) in the Work Product. Work Product, to the extent permitted by law, shall be deemed "works made for hire" (as that term is defined in the United States Copyright Act). Supplier shall provide Bank of America upon request with all assistance reasonably required to register, perfect or enforce such right, title and interest, including providing pertinent information and, executing all applications, specifications, oaths, assignments and all other instruments that Bank of America shall deem necessary. Supplier shall enter into agreements with all of its Representatives and Subcontractors necessary to establish Bank of America's sole ownership in the Work Product. Bank of America acknowledges Supplier's and its licensors' claims of proprietary rights in preexisting works of authorship and other intellectual property ("Pre-existing IP") Supplier uses in its work pursuant to this Agreement. Bank of America claims all rights of the Pre-existing IP used in its work. Unless otherwise agreed in an Order, Supplier grants Bank of America a perpetual, worldwide, irrevocable, nonexclusive royalty free license to any Pre-existing IP embedded in the Work Product, which shall permit Bank of America and any transferee or sublicensee of Bank of America, subject to the restrictions in this Agreement, to make, use, import, reproduce, display, distribute, make derivative works and modify such Pre-existing IP as necessary or desirable for the use of the Work Product.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction within section 39.1. The original text acknowledges the Supplier's and its licensors' claims to pre-existing IP, and grants Bank of America a license to use it. The changed text removes this acknowledgement and asserts that 'Bank of America claims all rights of the Pre-existing IP used in its work'. This directly contradicts the later statement that 'Supplier grants Bank of America a perpetual, worldwide, irrevocable, nonexclusive royalty free license to any Pre-existing IP embedded in the Work Product'. You cannot simultaneously claim ownership of IP and be granted a license to it. The conflicting statements create a clear in-text contradiction regarding the ownership and usage rights of the pre-existing IP, causing uncertainty and potential unenforceability, according to the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.
